intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The remit governs the uAgents framework runtime (`uagents` + `uagents-core`) — the Python plumbing that gives every deployed agent a cryptographic identity and wallet, registers it on the Fetch.ai Almanac, and exchanges typed messages with other agents over authenticated channels. The runtime may sign and send outbound messages, register and refresh its Almanac record, and dispatch schema-validated inbound messages to typed handlers; it must never execute remote-supplied shell/code, write private keys or seeds to disk in plaintext, or expose administrative or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers. Binding the HTTP server to a non-loopback interface and enabling remote reachability of the inspector/admin endpoints require operator approval. Inbound envelopes from non-user agent senders must carry a verified signature, and replayed or expired envelopes must never be re-processed as fresh.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Pure-Python framework: `uagents-core` supplies the crypto primitives (`Identity` over `ecdsa` SECP256k1 in `identity.py`, the signed `Envelope` in `envelope.py`) and `uagents` supplies the runtime — an `ASGIServer` (`asgi.py`) built on uvicorn, a `Dispatcher` (`dispatch.py`), and the `Agent` orchestrator (`agent.py`) with key-value `KeyValueStore` persistence (`storage/__init__.py`). Inbound envelopes are pydantic-validated and, for non-user senders, signature-verified before dispatch to typed handlers. The server hardcodes `HOST = "0.0.0.0"` and `forwarded_allow_ips="*"`, guards reserved admin endpoints only by a spoofable loopback-IP check, enables the agent inspector by default, and — on the default seedless named-agent path — persists identity and wallet private keys as cleartext in `./private_keys.json`.

behavior_summary: The framework hands deployed agents a genuinely strong messaging-authentication primitive — every non-user envelope is ECDSA-verified and pydantic-validated before dispatch — but wraps it in an insecure default network and key-handling posture that the operator never opts into. The dominant pattern is that the security-relevant defaults invert the remit: the server binds to `0.0.0.0` and trusts `X-Forwarded-For` from any client, so the loopback-only guard on the inspector/admin endpoints (`/messages`, `/connect`, `/disconnect`) is bypassable by a spoofed `X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1`, and the seedless key path writes identity and wallet keys to a plaintext file. Signature verification also stops at authenticity: there is no replay or expiry enforcement anywhere, so a captured valid envelope re-dispatches its handler. The compound remote-admin chain and the plaintext key default are the two items to look at first.

rules.rule_text: Executing operating-system shell commands or arbitrary code on behalf of a remote message sender.
Writing agent private keys, wallet keys, or seed phrases to disk in plaintext form.
Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.
Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity.
Must not be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers.
Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.
Sensitive configuration secrets MUST NOT be embedded as literals in framework or agent source.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.
Dispatching an inbound message from a non-user agent sender whose envelope signature has not been verified.
Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh.
Treating an unauthenticated, sender-asserted identity as trusted for any security decision.
Maximum retries before escalation: bounded; registration and delivery retries must be finite.
Actions that should never be retried: processing of an envelope already seen in the same session (replay).

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Halt Agent and Alert Operator
rules.rule_text: An inbound signed envelope fails signature verification.

rules.section: Known Good Baseline — Normal Restart Cadence
rules.rule_text: Restarts must not change a seeded agent's identity or wallet address.

findings.summary: On the default seedless named-agent path the framework writes identity and wallet private keys as cleartext to ./private_keys.json.
findings.description: When an agent is created with a name but no seed, get_or_create_private_keys generates the identity and wallet keys and save_private_keys serializes them, unencrypted, to private_keys.json in the working directory. These keys control the agent's on-chain identity and wallet funds, and the remit forbids writing them to disk in plaintext.
policy_rule_text: Writing agent private keys, wallet keys, or seed phrases to disk in plaintext form.
Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.

evidence.snippet: save_private_keys() json.dumps {"identity_key","wallet_key"} to private_keys.json with no encryption (lines 113-127); get_or_create_private_keys persists on first use (130-150)
seedless branch calls get_or_create_private_keys(name) (585-592), making plaintext key persistence the default for any named agent without a seed

recommended_actions: Stop persisting raw private keys: derive keys from an operator-supplied seed/secret held outside the repo, or encrypt the key file with an OS keychain / secret-manager-backed key before writing.
At minimum restrict private_keys.json to 0600 and document that the seedless path is unsafe for production; prefer the seed-from-env path in `_initialize_wallet_and_identity`.

findings.summary: Inbound signed envelopes are authenticated but never checked for freshness, so a captured valid envelope can be replayed and its handler re-runs.
findings.description: Envelope.verify() confirms only the ECDSA signature over the digest; nothing enforces that expires has not passed or that a session/nonce has not been seen before. The ASGI handler dispatches immediately after verify and uses expires only to size a sync-query timeout, and the Dispatcher keeps no seen-envelope cache — the exact replay/expired-reuse the remit forbids.
policy_rule_text: Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh.
Actions that should never be retried: processing of an envelope already seen in the same session (replay).

evidence.snippet: verify() returns Identity.verify_digest(...) — signature only; _digest (93-111) includes expires/nonce but both are Optional and default None, and neither is validated against the clock

dispatch_msg called right after env.verify() with no expiry/replay check; expires used only at 388-393 to compute a sync-response timeout. Dispatcher (dispatch.py:133-146) has no seen-nonce/session dedup

recommended_actions: Reject envelopes whose expires timestamp is in the past before dispatch, and require expires on signed envelopes.
Add a bounded replay cache keyed on (sender, session, nonce/digest) and drop any envelope already seen, so a captured envelope cannot re-trigger its handler.

findings.summary: A remote caller can reach the inspector/admin endpoints by spoofing X-Forwarded-For, because the server binds publicly and trusts forwarded IPs while gating admin routes only on loopback.
findings.description: The reserved endpoints /messages, /connect and /disconnect are guarded solely by `"127.0.0.1" not in scope["client"]`, but the server binds to 0.0.0.0 and sets forwarded_allow_ips="*", so uvicorn overwrites scope["client"] with any client-supplied X-Forwarded-For — a remote attacker sending X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1 passes the guard and reads message history or drives Agentverse connect/disconnect. The inspector is enabled by default, and /agent_info is exempt from the guard entirely. This is the remit's named bad behavior of reaching an admin endpoint via a spoofed loopback source.
policy_rule_text: Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.
Must not be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers.
Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.
Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity.
Treating an unauthenticated, sender-asserted identity as trusted for any security decision.

evidence.snippet: reserved-endpoint guard `request_path in RESERVED_ENDPOINTS-{"/agent_info"} and "127.0.0.1" not in scope["client"]` (304-311) is defeated because serve() sets forwarded_allow_ips="*" (184) with HOST="0.0.0.0" (23)
enable_agent_inspector defaults True (305); /messages, /connect, /disconnect registered as REST handlers (490-525), exposing history read and Agentverse registration control over the spoofable guard

recommended_actions: Do not trust client-supplied forwarding headers for the loopback decision: set forwarded_allow_ips to a trusted proxy list (or bind loopback) and derive the admin gate from the real transport peer, not scope["client"].
Require authentication (operator token) on the inspector/admin endpoints and disable the inspector by default; include /agent_info in the guard.

findings.summary: The agent HTTP server binds to 0.0.0.0 by default with no loopback option and no operator approval gate for public exposure.
findings.description: HOST is a module-level constant "0.0.0.0" that serve() passes straight to uvicorn, with no configuration to bind loopback and no approval step. The remit classifies binding to a non-loopback interface as an action requiring human approval; here every agent is publicly reachable by default, which both widens the /submit attack surface and is the enabling condition for the remote-admin chain.
policy_rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface.

evidence.snippet: HOST = "0.0.0.0" module constant, consumed unconditionally by uvicorn.Config(host=HOST,...) in serve() (179-195); no bind-interface parameter exposed on Agent

recommended_actions: Default the bind to 127.0.0.1 and expose a `host`/bind parameter so exposing a public interface is an explicit, logged operator choice rather than the built-in default.

findings.summary: The server returns wildcard CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *) on every response with no per-origin restriction.
findings.description: serve() hardcodes Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with wildcard allow-headers on all responses. Credentials are disabled, which limits cookie-based abuse, but any web origin can script cross-origin requests against the agent, and the wildcard is applied uniformly rather than scoped to a configured origin.

evidence.snippet: uvicorn headers set Access-Control-Allow-Origin "*", Access-Control-Allow-Headers "*", Access-Control-Allow-Credentials "false" (185-190) for all responses

recommended_actions: Make the allowed CORS origin configurable and default it to no cross-origin access (or a specific operator-set origin) rather than a static wildcard.

findings.summary: Agent identity signing relies on the pure-Python ecdsa library, which offers no constant-time / side-channel guarantee for keys that control on-chain assets.
findings.description: Identity generation, signing and verification are built on the pure-Python ecdsa SECP256k1 implementation, whose own documentation states it is not hardened against timing/side-channel attacks. Because these keys govern the agent's on-chain identity and wallet, the choice of an unhardened signer is a supply-chain risk for the framework's highest-sensitivity secret. The dependency is upper-capped but the underlying implementation property remains.

evidence.snippet: dependency `ecdsa (>=0.19.0,<1.0)` — pure-Python signer used for identity keypair and signing in identity.py:94-108,137-164 (SigningKey.generate / sign_digest)
Identity.from_seed / generate build ecdsa.SigningKey over SECP256k1; sign_digest/verify_digest use the same library for envelope authentication

recommended_actions: Move identity signing to a constant-time SECP256k1 implementation (e.g. the coincurve/libsecp256k1 binding) for the identity keypair, or document the residual side-channel risk and the deployment contexts where it is acceptable.

findings.summary: Durable inbound message history is off by default, leaving no persistent record to reconstruct an incident on a deployed agent.
findings.description: EnvelopeHistory persists inbound messages only when store_message_history is enabled, which defaults to False, and otherwise keeps at most an in-memory cache that exists only when the inspector is on. Combined with free-form text logging, a deployed agent has no durable, structured record of the messages it processed, so replay or spoofing attempts cannot be reconstructed after the fact.

evidence.snippet: store_message_history defaults False (313); EnvelopeHistory built with use_storage=store_message_history and use_cache tied to inspector (399-406), so no durable message record exists by default

recommended_actions: Provide an opt-in structured, durable audit log of inbound envelope metadata (sender, session, digest, verification result) independent of the inspector, and recommend enabling it in production.

positives.title: Cryptographic envelope verification enforced on the agent-to-agent path
positives.description: Non-user agent envelopes are ECDSA signature-verified before dispatch, and handlers registered as signed reject user (unverified) senders, so spoofed agent identities are rejected before any handler runs.

positives.title: Pydantic schema validation on every inbound envelope and payload
positives.description: Each inbound request is validated against the Envelope schema and its payload parsed against the registered pydantic message model before dispatch, rejecting malformed input with a 400.

positives.title: Deterministic seed-derived keys with no disk persistence when a seed is supplied
positives.description: When the operator supplies a seed, identity and wallet keys are derived in memory and never written to disk, and a restart preserves the same address — the recommended env-seed path avoids the plaintext key file.

positives.title: Dependency lockfiles committed with upper-bounded version caps
positives.description: Both packages ship lockfiles and cap most dependencies with an upper bound (e.g. pydantic >=2.8,<3.0), reducing dependency-confusion and silent-major-bump exposure.

rows.purpose: operational and warning messages (server start, failed envelope verification, registration status)
agent key-value state persistence (and optional message history when store_message_history enabled)

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The runtime has one real, operative control class — cryptographic envelope verification plus pydantic schema validation on the agent-to-agent path — but its default posture across the rest of the framework sits at the ad-hoc/partial boundary. Zero Trust is undercut by three defaults that contradict the remit: a public `0.0.0.0` bind, a spoofable loopback guard on admin endpoints, and plaintext private-key persistence; there is no replay/expiry enforcement on signed messages. Supply chain is partial (upper-bounded version caps and committed lockfiles, but a pure-Python `ecdsa` signer and one uncapped dependency), red-team evidence is limited to unit tests, and monitoring is present but unstructured with inbound history off by default.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The runtime dispatches only to developer-registered typed handlers matching a schema digest and rejects unknown paths, which is a real domain boundary, but reserved admin endpoints are exposed by a bypassable guard and the server has no bind-scope restriction by default.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Inbound payloads are validated against declared pydantic message schemas before dispatch (`asgi.py`, `agent.py:_process_single_message`), but the framework is model-agnostic plumbing with no content-origin labeling and message history can accumulate untrusted sender content when enabled.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Envelope signature verification and schema validation are operative on the inbound path, but three default-posture gaps (public `0.0.0.0` bind, `forwarded_allow_ips="*"` making the loopback admin guard spoofable, plaintext private-key persistence) plus the absence of any replay/expiry enforcement leave core trust boundaries unenforced.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Dependencies are floor-pinned with upper caps and lockfiles are committed (`uv.lock`, `poetry.lock`), but the core identity signer relies on the pure-Python `ecdsa` library with no constant-time guarantee and `uagents-core` is depended on with no upper bound.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A unit-test suite exists (`tests/test_msg_verify.py`, `test_server.py`) but there is no evidence of adversarial testing — no envelope-spoofing, replay, or admin-bypass tests — and `SECURITY.md` is a disclosure policy, not a testing practice.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Logging infrastructure is present via `get_logger`, but logs are free-form text with no structured action schema and durable inbound message history is off by default (`store_message_history=False`), leaving no durable record to reconstruct an incident.
